title: Multimaterial 3d-printing with functional fiber
abstract: In a method for printing a three dimensional structure, a continuous- length of fiber that includes, interior to a surface of the fiber, a plurality of different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional domain, with at least two electrical conductors disposed -in the functional domain in electrical contact with at least one functional domain material, is dispensed through a single heated nozzle. After sections of the length of fiber are dispensed from the heated nozzle, the sections are fused together in an arrangement of a three dimensional structure. The structure can thereby include a continuous length of fiber of least three different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional de-vice, with the continuous length of fiber disposed as a plurality of fiber sections that are each in a state of material fusion with another fiber section in a spatial arrangement of the structure.
claims:
Claims 1. A method for printing a three dimensional structure comprising: dispensing through a single heated nozzle a continuous length of fiber that includes, interior to a surface of the fiber, a plurality of different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional domain with at least two electrical conductors disposed in the functional domain in electrical contact with at least one functional domain material; and fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections of the length of fiber are dispensed from the heated nozzle, in an arrangement of a three dimensional structure. 2. The method of claim 1 wherein dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a heated nozzle comprises feeding said fiber length into an inlet to the heated nozzle and outputting said fiber length out of an outlet of the heated nozzle, while heating the outlet of the heated nozzle to an outlet temperature and maintaining the inlet to the heated nozzle at an inlet temperature that is no more than about 25% of the outlet temperature. 3. The method of claim 1 wherein the single heated nozzle comprises a nozzle channel that has a nozzle channel inlet and nozzle channel outlet, and a heating tube that has a heating tube inlet adjacent to the nozzle channel outlet and a heating tube outlet, and further comprising, while dispensing said fiber length through the heated nozzle, heating the heating tube inlet to a heating tube inlet temperature while maintaining the nozzle channel outlet at a nozzle channel outlet temperature that is no more than 40% of the heating tube inlet temperature. 4. The method of claim 1 further comprising, while dispensing said fiber length through the single heated nozzle, heating a fiber surface 

 material disposed at the surface of said fiber, along said length of fiber, to a critical fusing temperature that causes the surface fiber material to fuse to itself during arrangement of a three dimensional structure. 5. The method of claim 1 further comprising, while dispensing said fiber length through the single heated nozzle, heating a fiber surface material that is disposed at the surface of said fiber, along said fiber length, and that includes polymer chains, to a fiber surface temperature that is at least as high as a critical fusing temperature that causes interdiffusion of said polymer chains across fusion lines that are formed between fiber sections during arrangement of a three dimensional structure. 6. The method of claim 1 wherein the fiber includes a fiber surface layer of thermoplastic polymer material having a glass transition temperature and wherein dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a heated nozzle comprises feeding said fiber length into an inlet port of the heated nozzle while maintaining the inlet port at an inlet port temperature that is less than the thermoplastic polymer material glass transition temperature. 7. The method of claim 1 further comprising, while dispensing said fiber length through the single heated nozzle, maintaining the at least two electrical conductors in a solid, un-melted, state. 8. The method of claim 1 wherein said interior functional domain has a cross sectional diameter extending between outer edges of functional elements that are disposed interior to the fiber surface and that are formed 

 of functional domain materials different than a fiber material at the fiber surface; and further comprising, while dispensing said fiber length through the single heated nozzle, maintaining the functional domain materials at a functional domain temperature that is less than about a glass transition temperature of materials in said functional domain. 9. The method of claim 1 further comprising first forming the fiber, including a plurality of different materials that are each disposed interior to the fiber surface along the fiber length, before dispensing the fiber. 10. The method of claim 1 further comprising first forming the fiber, including at least three different materials that are each disposed at interior regions of the fiber and that each extend along the fiber length, the three different materials including at least one semiconducting material, before dispensing the fiber. 11. The method of claim 1 further comprising first thermally drawing a fiber preform into the fiber to be dispensed and fused. 12. The method of claim 1 further comprising first thermally drawing a fiber preform into a fiber including electrically conducting, electrically semiconducting, and electrically insulating materials, to be dispensed and fused. 13. The method of claim 1 further comprising first arranging a fiber preform that includes at least three different preform materials and then 

 thermally drawing the fiber preform into the fiber to be dispensed and fused. 14. The method of claim 1 further comprising first arranging a fiber preform that includes a semiconducting material, and then thermally drawing the fiber preform into the fiber to be dispensed and fused. 15. The method of claim 1 wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises layering sections of the length of fiber in an arrangement of a three dimensional structure. 16. The method of claim 1 wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into a hollow structure having solid structural walls. 17. The method of claim 1 wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into a solid object. 18. The method of claim 1 further comprising a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain that includes at least one an element which produces an output signal in an in-fiber electrical conductor in response to an external stimulus, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated 

 nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that produces an output signal in response to an external stimulus. 19. The method of claim 1 further comprising a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain that includes electronic devices distributed along the length of the fiber, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that includes electronic devices distributed over the object. 20. The method of claim 1 further comprising a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain that emits light, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that emits light. 21. The method of claim 1 a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain that emits light at separated pixel sites along the fiber length, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that produces an output that emits light at separated pixel sites of the object. 22. The method of claim 1 further comprising a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain that detects light, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that detects light. 23. The method of claim 1 a first step of forming a fiber including a functional domain comprising an electrical battery and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into an object that produces an output that comprises a battery. 24. The method of claim 1 further comprising a first step of forming a fiber including a piezoelectric functional domain, and wherein fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections are dispensed from the heated nozzle, comprises arranging fiber sections into a piezoelectric object. 25. The method of claim 1 wherein dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a single heated nozzle comprises dispensing the length of fiber through a nozzle having an inner nozzle diameter that is at least as large as an outer fiber diameter along the length of fiber. 26. The method of claim 1 wherein dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a single heated nozzle comprises dispensing the length of fiber through a nozzle having an inner nozzle diameter that is at most 14% greater than an outer fiber diameter along the length of fiber. 27. The method of claim 1 further comprising, while dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a single heated nozzle, maintaining constant external fiber dimensions along the length of fiber. 28. A method for printing a three dimensional structure comprising: dispensing through a single heated nozzle a continuous length of fiber that includes, interior to a surface of the fiber, at least three different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional device; and fusing together sections of the length of fiber, after said sections of the length of fiber are dispensed from the heated nozzle, in an arrangement of a three dimensional structure. 29. The method of claim 28 further comprising, while dispensing a continuous length of fiber through a single heated nozzle, maintaining constant external fiber dimensions along the length of fiber. 30. A nozzle for heating a fiber, the nozzle comprising: a nozzle channel having a nozzle channel length between a nozzle channel inlet and a nozzle channel outlet, the nozzle channel length having a substantially circular cross section and an inner nozzle channel diameter; a heating tube mechanically connected to the nozzle channel outlet, the heating tube having a heating tube length between a heating tube inlet and a heating tube outlet, the heating tube having a substantially circular cross section and having an inner heating tube diameter; a source of heat disposed at the heating tube for heating the heating tube; and the heating tube length being no greater than about 10% of a nozzle length that includes the nozzle channel length and the heating tube length. 31. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the heating tube length is no greater than about three times the inner heating tube diameter. 32. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the heating tube length is no greater than the inner heating tube diameter. 33. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the nozzle channel comprises polytetrafluoroethylene. 34. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the nozzle channel comprises a material that is electrically insulating and thermally insulating. 35. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the heating tube comprises a material that is electrically conducting and thermally conducting. 36. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the inner diameter of the heating tube and the inner diameter of the nozzle channel are each between about 0.1 mm and about 1.0 mm in diameter. 37. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the heating tube comprises stainless steel. 38. The nozzle of claim 30 wherein the source of heat disposed at the heating tube for heating the heating tube comprises at least one turn of electrically conducting wire connected to a circuit that passes electrical current through the wire. 39. A structure comprising: a continuous length of fiber; said continuous length of fiber comprising a plurality of different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional domain with at least two electrical conductors disposed in electrical contact with materials in the functional domain; and said continuous length of fiber disposed as a plurality of fiber sections that are each in a state of material fusion with another fiber section in a spatial arrangement of a structure, 40. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises at least three different materials. 41. The structure of claim 39 further comprising external electrical connections to said electrical conductors. 42. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises at least one electrically conductor provided as a conducting material selected from the group consisting of copper, tungsten, gold, silver, tin, aluminium, cadmium-copper, zirconium-copper, nickel-copper, a tin-lead alloy, electrically conducting polyethylene, and electrically conducting polycarbonate. 43. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises at least one semiconducting material. 44. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises at least one semiconducting material selected from the group consisting of silicon and germanium. 45. The structure of claim 39 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a hollow structure including said plurality of fiber sections arranged as structural walls. 46. The structure of claim 39 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a solid structure including said plurality of fiber sections as bulk structural material. 47. The structure of claim 39 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a layered stack of a plurality of fiber sections. 48. The structure of claim 39 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a layered stack of a plurality of fiber sections disposed as walls of a structure. 49. The structure of claim 39 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a non-planar structure including said plurality of fiber sections. 50. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes a sequence of spatially separated functional elements and 

 wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including spatially separated functional elements. 51. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes a sequence of spatially separated functional particles and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including spatially separated particles. 52. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes a light emitting in-fiber functional domain along the continuous length of fiber, and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure having at least one light emitting path in said structure. 53. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes an electroluminescent in-fiber domain along the continuous length of fiber, and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises an electroluminescent structure. 54. The structure of claim 53 wherein said electroluminescent domain includes a phosphor material selected from the group consisting of Cu- doped ZnS, yittrium aluminium garnet (YAG), manganese, aluminium, doped zinc-cadmium-sulphide (ZnCdS), and doped zinc-cadmium-sulfur- selenide (ZnCdSSe). 55. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes an electrical battery in-fiber domain along the continuous length 

 of fiber, and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure having an electrical power output. 56. The structure of claim 55 wherein said electrical battery in-fiber domain includes an anode material selected from the group consisting of Li2Ti03, lithium, a tin-based metal alloy, graphite, and silicon nanoparticles and includes a cathode material selected from the group consisting of LiMn 2 O 4 , LiTi 2 O 4 , LiV 2 O 4 , and LiFePO 4 . 57. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes an in-fiber piezoresistive domain along the continuous length of fiber, and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a piezoelectric structure. 58. The structure of claim 57 wherein said piezoresistive in-fiber functional domain comprises a piezoelectric material selected from the group consisting of oly(vinylidene fluoride), barium titanate, lead titanate, lead zirconate titanate, and zinc oxide. 59. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes at least one in-fiber functional domain that is responsive to external contact of a surface region of said fiber, and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure that is responsive to contact of said structure. 60. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises an in-fiber functional domain including a temperature-sensitive material that is in electrical contact with at least one electrical conductor; and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including said temperature-sensitive material in electrical contact with said at least one electrical conductor and an external electrical contact to said at least one electrical conductor. 61. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes a plurality of separated spheres that are each in electrical contact with at least one of said electrical conductors; and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including a plurality of separated spheres, wherein each of said spheres comprises a sphere of at least one of a metal and a semiconductor. 62. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises a functional domain including a photodetecting material that is in electrical contact with at least one of said electrical conductors; and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including said photodetecting material in electrical contact with at least one of said one electrical conductors and an external electrical contact to at least one of said electrical conductors. 63. The structure of claim 62 wherein said functional domain including a photodetecting material includes a chalcogenide semiconducting material. 64. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber comprises a functional domain including a chemically-sensitive material that is in electrical contact with at least one of said electrical conductors; and wherein said spatial arrangement comprises a structure including said chemically-sensitive material, in electrical contact with at least one of said electrical conductors and an external electrical contact to at least one of said electrical conductors. 65. The structure of claim 39 wherein said continuous length of fiber includes a thermoplastic polymer material. 66. The structure of claim 65 wherein said thermoplastic polymer material comprises a material selected from the group consisting of polycarbonate (PC), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), polylactic acid (PLA), a Cyclic Olefin Copolymer (COC), Polysulfone (PSU), Polyetherimide (PEI), Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), Polystyrene (PS), Polyvinyl Alchol (PVA), Nylon, Polyethersulfone (PES), and a thermoplastic elastomer. 67. A structure comprising: a continuous length of fiber; said continuous length of fiber comprising at least three different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional device; and said continuous length of fiber disposed as a plurality of fiber sections that are each in a state of material fusion with another fiber section in a spatial arrangement of a structure. 68. The structure of claim 67 further comprising at least one electrical conductor disposed in the functional domain in electrical contact with at least one functional domain material. 69. A filament comprising: a continuous length of fiber; said continuous length of fiber including an interior region comprising at least three different materials arranged as an in-fiber functional device; a cladding region comprising a cladding material having a cladding glass transition temperature, said cladding region surrounding the interior region along said continuous length of fiber; a fusion domain region comprising a fusion domain material having a fusion domain material glass transition temperature, said fusion domain region surrounding the cladding region; and wherein said cladding region glass transition temperature is at least about 50 Â°C greater than said fusion domain material glass transition temperature. 70. The filament of claim 69 wherein said fusion domain region is substantially circular in cross-section and said cladding region is substantially non-circular in cross-section 71. The filament of claim 69 wherein said fusion domain region material is a thermoplastic material selected from the group consisting of polycarbonate (PC), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), polylactic acid (PLA), a Cyclic Olefin Copolymer (COC), Polysulfone (PSU), Polyetherimide (Î¡ÎÎ), Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), Polystyrene (PS), Polyvinyl Alchol (PVA), Nylon, Polyethersulfone (PES), and a thermoplastic elastomer. 72. The filament of claim 69 wherein said fusion domain region is discontinuous along said length of fiber.
